cues.x: The Power of Habit, by Charles Duhigg.
Let me start with a small confession, one that Charles Duhigg himself makes near the end of this book, because it captures the whole thing in miniature.
Every single afternoon, for years, he would get up from his desk, walk to the cafeteria in his office building, and buy himself a chocolate chip cookie.
He would eat it, chat with a few colleagues near the counter, and go back to work.
Over time he noticed he had gained several pounds, and his wife made a comment about it, the kind of comment that lodges in your ribs.
So Duhigg, a reporter by trade, decided to investigate himself.
Why was he doing this?
He assumed he was hungry, or that he craved sugar.
But when he actually studied his own behavior, he discovered something surprising, and that surprise is the doorway into everything this book has to teach.
Hold onto that cookie.

We are going to come back to it, and by the time we do, you will understand not just why he ate it, but how he stopped, and how you can rewire almost any pattern in your own life using the very same logic.
The Power of Habit came out in two thousand and twelve, and it became one of those rare books that genuinely changes how you see ordinary life.
Duhigg's central claim is deceptively simple.
He argues that habits are not a minor footnote to human behavior.
They are the main event.
Researchers at Duke University found that something like forty percent of the actions people take on any given day are not actual decisions at all.
They are habits.
Think about what that means.
Nearly half of what you do today, you will do without deciding to do it.

The route you drive to work, the order in which you shower, the way you unlock your phone and immediately open a certain app, the words you use to greet people, whether you reach for a snack when you sit down to watch television.
You experience yourself as a conscious captain steering the ship, but for a huge fraction of the day, the ship is steering itself.
And Duhigg's argument is that this is not a bug.
It is a feature.
It is one of the most brilliant tricks your brain ever learned.
Understanding that trick is the first step to taking the wheel back when you want it.
To understand why the brain builds habits in the first place, we have to go to the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, and we have to meet some rats.
In the nineteen nineties, researchers there were studying the brains of rats by placing them in a T shaped maze.
Imagine a track shaped like the letter T.
The rat starts behind a partition at the bottom of the T.
There is a loud click as the partition slides open.
The rat then wanders up the corridor, sniffing, scratching at the walls, exploring in that aimless, twitchy way rats do.
Somewhere down one arm of the T, there is a piece of chocolate.
The rat's job, over many, many repetitions, is to learn to turn the right way and find the chocolate.
Now here is the part that matters.
The scientists had implanted tiny sensors in the rats' brains, so they could watch neural activity in real time as the animal moved through the maze.
The first time a rat goes through, its brain is working furiously.
As it wanders up the corridor, brain activity is high and busy the entire way.
The animal is processing everything: the smell of the walls, the feel of the floor, the search for the treat.
It is thinking hard, the way you think hard the first time you drive to a new address.
But here is the beautiful thing.
As the researchers ran the same rat through the same maze hundreds of times, the pattern of brain activity changed dramatically.
The mental effort dropped.
The animal stopped sniffing the walls and stopped scratching.
It just barreled down the corridor and turned correctly, faster and faster, and the parts of its brain associated with thinking and decision making went quiet.
The behavior had become automatic.
It had been handed off to a small, ancient, golf-ball-sized structure buried deep in the center of the brain called the basal ganglia.
This is one of the oldest parts of our neurology, the part we share with fish and reptiles, and it turns out to be the place where habits are stored and run.
But activity did not disappear completely.
And this is the crucial detail, the detail that unlocks the whole framework.
When the researchers looked closely, they saw that brain activity now spiked at only two moments.
It spiked right at the beginning, at the sound of the click when the partition opened.
And it spiked right at the end, the moment the rat reached the chocolate.
In the vast middle stretch, while the rat was actually running the maze, the brain was nearly silent.
Picture that shape in your mind: a spike at the start, a long flat quiet valley in the middle, and a spike at the end.
That shape is the anatomy of a habit.
The brain has figured out that it does not need to expend energy on the routine itself once it has learned it.
It only needs to pay attention at the trigger and at the payoff.
Everything in between runs on autopilot.
This gives us the framework at the heart of the book, and I want you to be able to picture it as a loop, because that is exactly what it is.
Imagine a circle, a closed loop with three parts.
First comes the cue.
The cue is the trigger, the click of the partition, the thing that tells your brain to switch into automatic mode and which habit to run.
Second comes the routine.
This is the behavior itself, the running of the maze, the thing you actually do.
And third comes the reward.
This is the payoff, the chocolate, the thing that tells your brain, yes, this loop is worth remembering for next time.
Cue, routine, reward.
Cue, routine, reward.
Around and around.
That circle is the fundamental unit of a habit, and it is happening inside you dozens of times a day.
Something triggers you, you run a behavior, you get a payoff, and your brain files the whole thing away so it can run it again without bothering you.
Now if the loop were only those three pieces, it would be fragile.
Something has to give the loop its power, something has to make it grab hold of you and pull.
And that something is the fourth ingredient, the engine that drives the whole machine.
It is craving.
To understand craving, we leave the rats and we meet a monkey named Julio.
Julio was part of research by a scientist named Wolfram Schultz, who was studying the brain chemical dopamine, which is deeply tied to pleasure and reward.
The setup was simple.
Julio sat in front of a screen.
When certain colored shapes flashed on the screen, if Julio pulled a lever, a drop of sweet blackberry juice would land on his tongue.
At first, Julio ignored the screen.
But gradually he learned the pattern: shapes, lever, juice.
And the scientists watched his brain.
In the beginning, Julio's dopamine, that spike of pleasure and reward, arrived at the moment the juice hit his tongue.
That makes intuitive sense.
The reward feels good, so the pleasure chemical fires when the reward arrives.
But as Julio learned the pattern, something remarkable happened.
The dopamine spike moved.

It moved earlier and earlier, until eventually it was firing not when the juice arrived, but the instant the shapes appeared on the screen, before he had even pulled the lever, before a single drop had touched his tongue.
His brain had begun to anticipate the reward.
It was firing off pleasure in expectation.
And once that anticipation was in place, Julio's brain had, in effect, started to want.
It had developed a craving.
The scientists proved it with a cruel little twist.
Once Julio was fully trained and expecting his juice, they sometimes showed the shapes and then withheld the juice.
And when the juice did not come, Julio's brain showed a pattern that neuroscientists associate with something like desire and frustration and disappointment.
He did not merely miss a treat.
He suffered from the absence of something his brain now craved.
That is the engine.
Craving is what turns a simple sequence into a compulsion.
When you see the cue, your brain does not calmly note that a reward might follow.
It begins to anticipate and to want that reward, and that wanting is what pulls you through the routine.
This is why the smell of doughnuts as you walk past a bakery makes your mouth water and your feet slow down.
This is why the little buzz of your phone makes your hand twitch toward your pocket before you have even thought about it.
The cue triggers a craving for the reward, and the craving drags you through the routine to get it.
Cue, craving, routine, reward.
Once you understand craving, you understand why habits feel so much stronger than mere intentions.
You are not fighting a behavior.
You are fighting a wanting that has been wired directly into the oldest part of your brain.
Now, if you want to see this science turned into a fortune, meet Claude Hopkins.
Hopkins was one of the most brilliant advertising men in American history, working in the early nineteen hundreds.
In the early nineteen twenties, an old friend approached him with a new product called Pepsodent, a toothpaste.
Hopkins was hesitant, because at the time, brushing your teeth was not a national habit.
Most Americans simply did not do it.
Beautiful, healthy teeth were considered a matter of luck.
Hopkins needed to find a way to make an entire nation brush their teeth every single day, and he needed to do it by building a habit loop.
So he went looking for a cue and a reward.
For the cue, he found his answer in dental textbooks.
He read about a naturally occurring film that builds up on your teeth, a coating you can feel with your tongue.
Everyone has it.
It is more or less harmless, and it comes off whether you brush or not.
But Hopkins did not care about the biology.
He cared about the trigger.
He built an entire advertising campaign around that film.
Just run your tongue across your teeth, the ads said, and you will feel it, that dingy film.
That was the cue, and it was genius, because the film is always there.
Anyone who read the ad could immediately feel it with their own tongue, which meant everyone now carried a permanent trigger around inside their own mouth, a cue they could not escape.
The ads promised that Pepsodent would remove this ugly film and give you a beautiful smile.
But the cue and the promise alone would not have been enough to make the habit stick.
The real stroke of brilliance, and Hopkins may not even have fully understood why it worked, was in the reward.
Hopkins added certain ingredients to Pepsodent, notably mint oil and citric acid, that created a cool, tingling sensation on the tongue and gums.
That tingle served no cleaning purpose whatsoever.
It was purely a sensation.
But it turned out to be the craveable reward that locked the whole loop into place.
People came to associate that clean, cool, tingling feeling with a clean mouth.
And soon they craved the tingle.
If they forgot to brush, they missed it.
Later, other toothpaste makers who left out those tingling agents found their products failed, and they had to add the tingle back in.
The lesson Hopkins stumbled onto is one of the deepest in the book.
To create a new habit, you need a cue, and you need a reward, but you especially need a reward that people come to crave.
Pepsodent went from a risky bet to one of the best-known products on earth, and within a decade, the majority of Americans were brushing their teeth every day.
Hopkins had not just sold toothpaste.
He had installed a habit in a nation.
Now let me tell you about a product that got this wrong before it got it right, because the contrast is illuminating.
In the nineteen nineties, the company Procter and Gamble developed something genuinely miraculous, a spray called Febreze.
It was based on a chemistry that actually eliminated bad odors from the air and from fabric, rather than just covering them up.
This was a real breakthrough.
The scientists were thrilled.
The marketers built campaigns around the obvious use case: getting rid of terrible smells.
They showed a woman bothered by her smoking section at a restaurant, spraying Febreze to remove the stink.
And the product flopped.
It sat on shelves.
Sales were dismal.
The company was on the verge of killing it entirely.
So they sent researchers out to interview people and figure out what was going wrong.
They visited a woman in Phoenix whose home was immaculate.
When they asked about smells, she was puzzled.
She did not think her house smelled bad at all.
And that was the problem, generalized across the whole market.
The people whose homes smelled the worst, the ones with nine cats or heavy smoke, had usually gone nose-blind to their own odors.
They did not notice the smell, so they felt no cue to spray.
And the people whose homes smelled fine, well, they had no bad smell to remove either.
Febreze had been designed as a reward for a cue, the bad smell, that its best customers could not detect.
There was no trigger, so there was no habit, so there were no sales.
Then the researchers noticed something almost by accident.
They watched a woman cleaning her house.
She would make a bed, and then, as a little finishing flourish, she would spritz the freshly made bed with Febreze.
She would clean a room, and give it a spray at the end.
For her, Febreze was not about removing a stink.
It was a little reward at the end of cleaning, a pleasant scent that marked the job as done, the way you might smile at a tidy room.
That reframed everything.

The company reformulated the product to have a pleasant, noticeable perfume of its own, rather than being scentless, and they relaunched the advertising to show Febreze as the happy final step of cleaning: you tidy up, and then you spritz, and the fresh scent is your reward.
The cue became the act of cleaning.
The reward became that pleasant smell that signals a job well done.
And Febreze exploded into a product that earns more than a billion dollars a year.
Same chemistry, completely different fortune, and the difference was building a proper habit loop with a reward people actually craved.
So far we have been talking about creating habits.
But most of us are more desperate to change the bad habits we already have.
And here Duhigg gives us what he calls the Golden Rule of habit change, and it is the single most practical idea in the book, so let me say it clearly.
You cannot truly extinguish a habit.
You can, however, change it.
And the way you change it is this: you keep the same cue, and you keep the same reward, but you insert a new routine in between them.
The old cue still fires.
The old reward still lands.
But the behavior in the middle, the routine, is swapped out for a better one.
Keep the cue, keep the reward, change the routine.
That is the Golden Rule.
Consider how this played out in professional football with a coach named Tony Dungy.
For years, Dungy had a philosophy that other teams thought was almost naive.
He believed that winning came not from a thick playbook full of complicated schemes, but from making his players' reactions so automatic that they did not have to think at all.
In the split second of a play, a thinking player is a slow player.
Dungy wanted his defense to respond to specific visual cues on the field, the position of a receiver's foot, the tilt of a quarterback's hips, with instant, habitual, unthinking reactions.
He was not installing new athletes.
He was rewiring their existing habits.

He kept the cues, the things the players already saw on the field, and he kept the rewards, the satisfaction of a good play and winning, but he drilled a new routine, a new automatic response, in between.
Dungy took the Indianapolis Colts and eventually, after years, won the Super Bowl.
But Duhigg is honest about something important here.
Dungy's method worked brilliantly most of the time, but it tended to fall apart under the highest pressure, in the most stressful moments of the biggest games.
And that hints at a missing ingredient, one that we find most clearly in a completely different arena: recovery from addiction.

Alcoholics Anonymous is one of the most successful behavior change programs in human history, and yet for a long time nobody could fully explain why it worked, because it had no scientific basis and its founders were not neuroscientists.
But when you look at it through the lens of the habit loop, its genius becomes clear.
A person addicted to alcohol has a powerful habit loop.
There is a cue, often stress or loneliness or a certain time of evening.
There is a routine, drinking.
And there is a reward, but here is the subtle part, the reward is often not the alcohol itself.
Frequently the real reward is relief, or escape, or companionship, or the simple release of tension.
Alcoholics Anonymous, without using this vocabulary, attacks the routine while preserving the cue and the reward.
When the cue of stress hits, instead of drinking, the person is taught a new routine: go to a meeting, call your sponsor, talk to another person who understands.
And that new routine delivers a similar reward, the relief, the companionship, the release, without the alcohol.
But the Golden Rule alone, Duhigg shows, is often not quite enough, and Alcoholics Anonymous reveals what the missing ingredient is.
Researchers found that swapping the routine worked well until life delivered a serious blow: a death, a job loss, a crisis.
Under that kind of stress, the new routine would crack and people would relapse, just as Tony Dungy's players sometimes cracked in the biggest moments.
What separated the people who held on from the people who fell back was a single thing, and it was belief.
The people who kept their new habits under maximum stress were the ones who had come to genuinely believe that things could get better, that change was possible.
And crucially, belief was easiest to sustain not alone but in a group.
When your own faith flickered, the community carried it for you.
You could look around the room and see people who had made it, and borrow their belief until yours returned.
This is the deep lesson.

To change a habit and have it survive real adversity, you need to keep the cue and reward and swap the routine, yes, but you also need to believe change is possible, and that belief is far sturdier when it is held by a community around you.
Now we come to what may be the most powerful idea in the entire book, the idea of the keystone habit.
Not all habits are created equal.
Some habits are ordinary.
But a few special habits, when you change them, set off a chain reaction that reorganizes everything else.
These are keystone habits, named after the wedge-shaped stone at the top of an arch that holds all the other stones in place.
Change the keystone, and the whole structure shifts.
And the story Duhigg uses to illustrate this is so striking that it deserves telling in full.
In nineteen eighty-seven, a man named Paul O'Neill became the chief executive of the Aluminum Company of America, known as Alcoa.
It was an old, enormous, somewhat troubled industrial company.
When O'Neill held his first big meeting with Wall Street investors and stock analysts, they expected the usual talk about profit margins and inventory and market strategy.
Instead, O'Neill stood up and started talking about worker safety.
He said his single priority was to make Alcoa the safest company in America.
He wanted to drive the rate of workplace injuries to zero.
He barely mentioned profits at all.
The investors were horrified.
One of them reportedly rushed out of the room to call his clients and tell them to sell their Alcoa stock immediately, convinced that the board had put a crazy person in charge.
That investor could not have been more wrong.
Paul O'Neill had chosen worker safety very deliberately, not because he was ignoring profits, but because he understood keystone habits, even if he did not use that phrase.
He understood that you cannot order thousands of people to suddenly become more profitable.
But you can rally almost anyone around not getting hurt.
And to genuinely make a factory safer, a whole cascade of other things has to change.
To prevent injuries, you have to understand exactly why they happen, which means you have to analyze your manufacturing processes in fine detail.
That analysis reveals inefficiencies and flaws you would otherwise never see.
To report and fix safety issues fast, you have to build new lines of communication, so that a worker on the floor can get a message straight up to senior management.
That new communication network then carries not just safety information but good ideas of every kind.
Managers who become excellent at spotting safety problems become excellent at spotting quality problems too.
Safety, in other words, was the keystone.
Pull on that one habit, and it dragged communication, analysis, quality, morale, and efficiency along with it.
And the results were astonishing.
Alcoa's safety record became one of the best in the world, with injury rates falling to a tiny fraction of the industry average.
But that was not all.
By the time Paul O'Neill retired, the company's annual net income was roughly five times higher than when he had arrived, and its market value had grown by billions of dollars.
The investor who ran out to sell had, in fact, bet against one of the greatest value creations in the company's history.
That is the power of a keystone habit.
You do not have to change everything at once.
You find the one habit that, when it shifts, pulls all the others along behind it.
In personal life, exercise is often a keystone habit.
People who start exercising regularly frequently find that, without trying, they start eating better, spending more wisely, procrastinating less, being more patient with their families.
They did not decide to overhaul their lives.
They changed one keystone, and the arch reorganized itself.
But changing habits and building new ones takes something we all feel we run short of, and that is willpower.

So Duhigg digs into what willpower actually is, and here we meet one of the most famous experiments in modern psychology, sometimes described as the radishes and cookies study, associated with the researcher Roy Baumeister.
Researchers brought people, one at a time, into a room that had been filled with the smell of freshly baked chocolate chip cookies.
On the table sat a plate of those warm cookies, and next to it, a bowl of plain radishes.
Half the participants were told they could eat the cookies.
The other half, the unlucky ones, were told they could only eat the radishes, and they had to resist the cookies.
Then the researchers left the room, so each radish-eater sat there alone, smelling cookies, forbidden to touch them, forcing down bitter radishes.
It took real willpower to resist.
Then came the real experiment.
Everyone was taken to another room and given a puzzle to solve.
What they did not know was that the puzzle was impossible, it could not actually be solved.
The researchers were not measuring whether people solved it.
They were measuring how long people would persist before giving up in frustration.
The people who had been allowed to eat cookies, who had not spent any willpower resisting temptation, worked on the impossible puzzle for a good while, around nineteen minutes on average.
But the people who had just exhausted themselves resisting the cookies gave up much faster, in about eight minutes.
They had less patience, less persistence, less self-control left.
The conclusion was profound: willpower is not simply a skill or a virtue.
It behaves like a muscle.
It gets tired.
When you spend it resisting temptation in one part of your life, you have less of it available for the challenges that come next.
This is why you make good food choices all day and then blow it at night when your willpower muscle is exhausted.
This is why decision after decision leaves you drained and prone to impulse.
But there is very good news buried in that finding.
If willpower is a muscle, then, like a muscle, it can be strengthened with practice.
And even better, the smartest move is to convert acts of willpower into habits, so they no longer cost willpower at all.
Once a behavior is automatic, running down in the quiet valley of the basal ganglia, it barely draws on your limited reserve of self-control.
And this is exactly how a great company like Starbucks turned an army of young, often inexperienced employees into consistently poised professionals.
Starbucks realized that its baristas would inevitably face stressful, willpower-draining moments, above all an angry, yelling customer.
In that moment, a frightened young employee's natural instinct might be to freeze, to snap back, or to fall apart.
So Starbucks did not just tell them to have more willpower.
They drilled specific habit routines for precisely these hard moments, so that the response would be automatic and would not require improvising under pressure.
One of these was a routine sometimes taught with the acronym LATTE, which is a lovely fit for a coffee company.
Each letter is a step.
L is for Listen, actually hear the customer out.
A is for Acknowledge, recognize their complaint, let them know you understand what went wrong.
T is for Take action, do something concrete to solve the problem.
The second T is for Thank, thank the customer for their patience or for pointing out the issue.
And E is for Explain, explain why the problem occurred, so it does not feel arbitrary.
Listen, Acknowledge, Take action, Thank, Explain.
Employees practiced this sequence over and over, role-playing difficult customers, until when a real furious customer appeared, the routine simply fired on its own.
What could have been a willpower-shattering ordeal became a smooth, almost automatic performance.
Starbucks had turned self-control from a scarce resource that gets depleted into a trained habit that runs on its own.
That is a lesson each of us can steal.
The moments where you most need willpower are usually predictable.
Plan the routine in advance, practice it, and let it become a habit, so that when the hard moment comes, you are not relying on willpower you may not have.
Now let me take you somewhere that might make you slightly uneasy, because habits are not just personal.
Companies study your habits, and they use them to sell to you, sometimes with unsettling precision.
This brings us to the retail giant Target and a statistician named Andrew Pole.
Target, like every big retailer, assigns each shopper a kind of identification number linked to their purchases, so it can track buying patterns over time.
Pole was given a fascinating and slightly delicate assignment.
The company knew that most people's shopping habits are deeply ingrained and hard to change.
You buy your usual brands at your usual store out of pure habit, and advertising rarely shakes you loose.
But there are certain moments in life when habits shatter and are suddenly up for grabs, when a person is willing to form entirely new shopping patterns.
And one of the biggest such moments in anyone's life is the arrival of a new baby.
A new parent is exhausted, overwhelmed, and desperate, and will suddenly buy all sorts of new things and form new store loyalties.
If Target could identify expectant mothers early, ideally before the baby arrived and before the competition knew, it could hook them as customers for years.
So Andrew Pole went digging through the data, and he found the patterns.
He discovered that by tracking purchases of around twenty-five specific products, he could assign a shopper a pregnancy prediction score, and even estimate a due date fairly accurately.
What were these telltale products?
Things like unscented lotion, which pregnant women often start buying in large quantities.
Certain nutritional supplements like calcium, magnesium, and zinc.
Extra-large bags of cotton balls.
Hand sanitizers and washcloths.
No single purchase meant much.
But the combination, the pattern across those roughly two dozen products, was like a signal flare.
Target could now identify pregnant customers with startling accuracy and start sending them coupons for baby products, timed to their stage of pregnancy, before most of their friends even knew.
And this is where the story takes its famous, cautionary turn.
As Duhigg tells it, a man walked into a Target store outside Minneapolis, angry, holding coupons that had been mailed to his teenage daughter.
The coupons were for baby clothes and cribs and nursery furniture.
The father demanded to know why Target was sending this to his high school daughter.
Were they trying to encourage her to get pregnant?
The store manager, who had no idea what the algorithm had done, apologized sincerely.
Then he called the family a few days later to apologize again.
And this time the father's tone had changed.
He was embarrassed.
He said he had had a talk with his daughter, and it turned out there were things going on in his house that he had not been aware of.
His daughter was, in fact, pregnant.
Target's data had detected her pregnancy before her own father did.
Now, Target learned an important lesson from cases like this, and it is a lesson about the psychology of habit and comfort.
When they sent people advertisements that were too accurate, ads made up entirely of pregnancy and baby products, it felt creepy and invasive, and it actually spooked customers away.
People do not like feeling watched.
So Target got clever.
They began hiding the baby ads inside otherwise ordinary flyers.
Next to a coupon for diapers, they would place a coupon for a lawnmower, or wine glasses, or something totally random.
To the pregnant shopper, it now looked like a normal, generic advertisement that just happened to contain a few things she needed, rather than a spotlight aimed directly at her secret.
And sales of baby products at Target boomed.
The takeaway for you as a person moving through the world is worth sitting with.
Your habits are legible to those who study your data, sometimes more legible than they are to you.
And the systems around you are carefully engineered to fit into your habit loops without you noticing.
Awareness of that is itself a small kind of freedom.
Habits do not stop at the level of the individual or even the corporation.

They scale all the way up to entire societies and movements, and to show this, Duhigg tells the story of Rosa Parks and the Montgomery bus boycott, one of the founding moments of the American civil rights movement.
In nineteen fifty-five, in Montgomery, Alabama, Rosa Parks, a black woman, refused to give up her bus seat to a white passenger, and she was arrested.
Now, here is a question Duhigg asks that most people never think about.
Parks was not the first black person in Montgomery to be arrested for refusing to give up a bus seat.
Others had been arrested before her for exactly the same act of defiance.
So why did Rosa Parks's arrest ignite a movement that changed history, while the others faded into forgotten footnotes?
The answer, Duhigg argues, lies in the social habits of a community, and in the different kinds of relationships that bind people together.
Sociologists talk about strong ties and weak ties.
Strong ties are your close relationships, your friends and family, the people you love.
Weak ties are your acquaintances, the wider web of people you know casually, people you nod to but are not intimate with.
Rosa Parks was special not because of her act, which others had performed, but because of who she was in the community.
She was deeply respected and widely connected.
She sang in the church choir, she was involved in local organizations, she knew and was loved by a huge range of people across the city, from the poor to the professional.
When she was arrested, it was not a stranger being arrested.
It was everyone's friend.
And because of her strong ties, her many close friendships, people who cared about her personally sprang into action immediately.
The movement began, powered first by the strong ties of friendship.
People joined the initial boycott because their friend had been wronged.
But friendship alone cannot sustain a boycott of an entire bus system for over a year, through hardship and intimidation and exhaustion.
Strong ties get it started, but they are too few to keep a whole city walking to work for month after month.
What sustained it was the second kind of tie, the weak ties, working through the powerful force of social peer pressure.
In a tightly woven community, there is an unspoken expectation about how you behave, and a real social cost to letting your neighbors down.
As the boycott spread, participating became a community expectation.

If your neighbors and your congregation and your acquaintances were all walking, all sacrificing, then you walked too, because the social pressure of the whole community made staying home and riding the bus feel like a betrayal.
Weak ties, the pressure of the wider community, pushed the movement past the point where a few close friendships could carry it.
And then came the final, decisive ingredient, the one that made the movement not just large but self-sustaining.

The organizers, led by a young minister named Martin Luther King Junior and others, gave the participants new habits, new patterns of behavior, and a new sense of identity, delivered largely through the churches.

The churches turned protest into a set of regular practices, meetings, songs, sermons, rhythms of action, so that participating in the movement became a habit woven into the fabric of daily and weekly life.
The movement gave people a new way of seeing themselves and new routines to live out that identity.
Once that happened, the movement no longer needed a single charismatic leader present at every moment to keep it going.
It ran on its own, the way a habit runs on its own, propelled by the community's new self-sustaining patterns.
Strong ties to start it, weak ties to grow it, and new self-sustaining habits to make it permanent.
That is the anatomy of how a social movement takes hold, and Duhigg argues it is the same loop logic operating at the scale of a whole society.
So now, at last, we can return to Charles Duhigg and his afternoon cookie, because his own little story is where all of this theory becomes a practical tool you can pick up and use tomorrow.
Remember, every afternoon he got up and bought and ate a chocolate chip cookie, and it was making him gain weight, and he wanted to stop.
So he did not just try to white-knuckle his way out of it with willpower, which we now know is a muscle that tires.
Instead, he treated himself like a science experiment and used the habit loop as his diagnostic tool.
And the method he used is exactly the method Duhigg offers you at the close of the book.
Let me walk you through it step by step, because this is the part you can actually apply to your own life.
The first step is to identify the routine.
This is usually the easy part, because the routine is the behavior you want to change, the thing you can plainly see yourself doing.
For Duhigg, the routine was clear: get up from the desk, walk to the cafeteria, buy a cookie, eat it.
That is the middle of the loop, the thing you want to swap out.
So name it plainly.
What exactly is the behavior you keep repeating that you wish you did not?
The second step, and this is the one most people skip, is to experiment with rewards, in order to discover what reward you are truly craving.
This is subtle, because the reward you assume you are after is very often not the real one.
Duhigg assumed his reward was the cookie itself, the sugar, the taste.
But he was not sure, so he ran experiments.
On one afternoon, when the urge hit, instead of getting a cookie, he went for a walk around the block and came back to his desk.
On another day, he bought a doughnut instead of a cookie and ate it at his desk, to test whether it was really about the sugar.
On another day, he got up but instead of going to the cafeteria he went to a colleague's desk and chatted for a few minutes, then returned.

Each time, after satisfying the urge in a different way, he would sit down and, on a piece of paper, jot down the first three things that came to mind, and then set an alarm for fifteen minutes later.
When the alarm rang, he would ask himself: do I still feel the urge to get a cookie?
He was hunting for which substitute actually made the craving go away.
And the answer surprised him.
The days he bought a doughnut, so he still had the sugar, the urge to go get a cookie came back anyway.
So it was not really about the sugar.
But on the days he got up and went to talk to a friend for a few minutes, he found that afterward, the urge to get a cookie had vanished.
That was the revelation.
The real reward he had been craving all along was not the sugar at all.
It was the social connection, the little break to talk to people.
The cookie was just the excuse his brain had attached to that craving.
He had been eating hundreds of calories every afternoon to satisfy a desire for a few minutes of chatting.
Once he knew the true reward, everything became solvable.
The third step is to isolate the cue.
Habits are triggered by a cue, but cues are slippery and hard to spot in the flood of daily life.
Here Duhigg gives us a wonderfully practical shortcut.
Researchers have found that almost every habitual cue falls into one of five categories.
When you feel the urge to run a habit, ask yourself five questions, drawn from these five categories.
What time is it?
Where am I?
What is my emotional state right now?
Who else is around me?
And what action did I just take right before this, what immediately preceded the urge?
Time, location, emotional state, other people, and the preceding action.
Nearly every cue you will ever hunt for hides in one of those five buckets.
So for several days, the moment the urge strikes, you quickly note down those five things.
Then you look for the pattern, the one variable that is consistent every time.
For Duhigg, when he tracked it, the cue turned out to be the time of day.
The urge reliably struck at a certain point in the afternoon, somewhere around three or three thirty.
It was not hunger, it was not a location, it was a time, and at that time his brain wanted a social break.
And now, with all three pieces identified, the routine, the true reward, and the cue, you simply apply the Golden Rule.
You keep the cue.
You keep the reward.
And you insert a new routine in between.
Duhigg made himself a plan.
He decided that at three thirty every afternoon, when the cue fired, he would get up and go find a friend and talk for a few minutes, and then return to his desk.
He kept the cue, that mid-afternoon moment.
He kept the reward, the social connection and the mental break.
But he swapped the routine, chatting with a colleague instead of eating a cookie.
And it worked.
The cookie habit faded, not through a heroic act of self-denial, but through intelligent redesign.
He gave his craving what it actually wanted, and the cookie, which had never been the point, simply fell away.
So let me draw all of these threads together, because they form one coherent picture of how human behavior really works, and one hopeful conclusion.
Around forty percent of what you do every day is not a decision but a habit, run automatically by the ancient basal ganglia so your conscious mind can rest.

Every one of those habits is a loop: a cue that triggers you, a routine that you run, and a reward that satisfies you, all of it powered by craving, the anticipatory wanting that pulls you through the loop before you have even thought about it, just as it pulled Julio the monkey toward his blackberry juice.
You saw how Claude Hopkins built a nation of tooth-brushers with a film to feel and a tingle to crave, and how Febreze failed until its makers made a pleasant scent the reward at the end of cleaning.

You saw the Golden Rule, keep the cue, keep the reward, change the routine, at work in Tony Dungy's Colts and in the meetings of Alcoholics Anonymous, and you learned that to hold a new habit under real stress you need belief, and that belief lives most strongly in community.
You saw keystone habits reshape an entire company when Paul O'Neill made worker safety the one thing that pulled everything else, and profits and market value, along with it.
You learned that willpower is a muscle that tires, proven by radishes and cookies, and that the wisest response is to turn willpower into trained habit, the way Starbucks did with its LATTE routine.
You saw your habits laid bare in Target's data, precise enough to spot a pregnancy before a father knew.
And you saw habits scale all the way up to a bus boycott in Montgomery, carried by strong ties, sustained by weak ties, and made permanent by new self-sustaining habits in the churches.
And here is the final message, the reason this book matters beyond its stories.

Once you truly understand that a habit is not a fixed feature of who you are, but a changeable loop of cue, routine, and reward, driven by a craving you can learn to see, then something shifts inside you.
The habit stops being your master and becomes a mechanism, a piece of machinery you can open up, inspect, and rebuild.
Duhigg is careful not to promise this is easy.
Some habits, especially the deepest ones, take enormous work, and belief, and support to change.
But he insists, and the science backs him, that they can be changed, because they are not destiny.
They are architecture.
And that knowledge is a double-edged gift.
It gives you freedom, the freedom to reshape the automatic patterns that make up so much of your life, to stop the afternoon cookie or start the morning run or transform how you react under pressure.
But it also gives you responsibility, because once you can see the loop, you can no longer pretend you are simply at its mercy.
You know now that the pattern can be rewritten.
The maze is yours to redraw.

So the next time you catch yourself in the middle of some routine you did not consciously choose, reaching for the phone, the snack, the sharp word, the old road, pause for just a moment and ask the three questions.
What is the cue that just triggered me?
What is the routine I am running?
And what is the reward I am truly after, underneath the surface behavior?
Because the moment you can name those three things, you are no longer a passenger in your own life.
You are holding the pen.
And that, in the end, is the real power of habit: not that our lives are ruled by patterns, but that once we understand those patterns, we get to decide what they will be.
